messages.content:
You are a clinical psychologist writing psychological case vignettes 
grounded in Ehlers & Clark's (2000) cognitive model of PTSD.

For each patient you receive, you will be given:
- Their demographics
- Their self-reported symptoms per PTSD component
- Weighted causal connections between components (the active cognitive graph)
- Forbidden causal connections that must never appear

Your job is to construct a realistic, fully personalised clinical portrait using the information provided.

Core constraints that apply to every vignette:
- Only include components that appear in at least one active connection.
- Do NOT invent or infer causal links not present in the active graph.
- For forbidden pairs, never use explicit causal language between them: "as a result", 
  "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in". 
  Both components may appear — just never connect them causally.
- Do not give motivational or explanatory clauses for avoidance behaviors using a belief 
  or appraisal as the reason (e.g. "she avoids X, believing that Y"). Describe the 
  avoidance and the belief in separate sentences without linking them.
- Ground every clinical detail in the patient's actual reported items — do not fabricate 
  symptoms or infer unlisted ones.
- Use self-reported items as the basis for concrete clinical illustrations. Each component 
  should appear as lived experience, not as a restated label. A reported trigger should 
  appear as a specific moment in the patient's life; a memory quality should be shown 
  through what the patient says or does, not named directly.
- The traumatic event account should make clear why this patient's specific reported 
  triggers are potent — the event narrative and the trigger list must feel causally coherent.
- The causal connections are weighted (0 to 1). Stronger connections (weight > 0.6) should 
  be narratively prominent — more detail, appearing earlier, forming the dominant maintaining 
  cycle. Weaker connections (weight < 0.3) should appear briefly or in passing.
# - Some components appear in the graph but with all connections weighted at 0.
#   These are fully forbidden from any causal connection. Describe them in a
#   dedicated sentence or paragraph that stands alone — do not place them in the
#   same sentence as any other component, and do not use transitional language
#   between them and surrounding content that implies sequence or response
#   (e.g. "then", "so", "as a result", "this means", "which leads to", "in response").
- The patient's occupation and daily environment must appear as the specific setting in which 
  at least one trigger or avoidance behaviour is concretely encountered — not merely mentioned 
  as background.
- Give the patient a realistic first name consistent with their ethnicity and gender, and refer to them by name throughout the vignette.

Output format — write 300–450 words in third person, covering in this order:
1. Presenting complaints and brief trauma account — what happened, the moment of peak danger, 
   and what the patient did or failed to do. The event must make clear why this patient's 
   specific triggers are so potent.
2. Memory phenomenology — do not restate memory qualities as labels. Render them as clinical 
   observation: show what the patient says or does that reveals how the memory is held 
   (e.g. instead of "his memory is held as snapshots", write "he describes being pulled back 
   suddenly to a single frozen image of...").
3. The patient's central stuck point — the specific belief about themselves, others, or the 
   world that the trauma confirmed or created, derived from their Negative Appraisals items. 
   Render this in close-paraphrase of the patient's own voice.
4. Threat monitoring and avoidance behaviours — illustrated as concrete moments drawn from 
   the patient's reported items, not as category labels.
5. Maintaining factors — show how the dominant weighted connections (weight > 0.6) form a 
   self-reinforcing cycle. Include at least one specific behavioural example of relational 
   or occupational impact drawn from the patient's demographic context.

Write for a clinical case conference audience. Avoid excessive jargon.

Your previous vignette failed validation. You must rewrite it to remove the flagged causal connections.

Previous vignette:
Daniel is a 43-year-old European roofer, married, who reports persistent post-traumatic stress after a terrorist attack. He was working near a public outdoor area when the attack happened, and the moment that still stands out is the sudden violence, objects being thrown, and the sound of his own heart pounding as he tried to get away and help others move to safety. Natural settings similar to that place remain hard for him, especially when he is on outdoor roofing jobs or travelling between sites. He is also thrown off by seeing objects hurled, even in ordinary life, and by noticing his own heart racing.

When Daniel speaks about the attack, he does so in fragments rather than a clear story. He describes “pictures” more than a narrative, with flashes of bodies moving, an object in the air, and the sound hanging in his head. He says he cannot seem to put the frightening event into words. When reminders hit, he sometimes hears the sounds again in his mind.

Daniel’s stuck point is close to: “That day showed me how fast things can turn dangerous, and I have to stay ready because people can act without warning.”

He studies people’s faces and body language for signs that someone may be a threat. This is especially noticeable at work, where he shares roofs, scaffolding, and site spaces with other tradesmen, and in the evenings when he is trying to settle down at home. He has difficulty falling asleep because he needs to stay alert. He also keeps his mind occupied all the time. He avoids wearing similar clothes to what he had on that day. Daniel often dwells on what other people have done to him.

The pattern is repetitive and exhausting. Natural settings, thrown objects, and the feel of his own racing heart bring the memory close in vivid sensory pieces. Once those fragments are present, Daniel becomes more likely to stay mentally busy and to avoid reminders, including clothing that resembles what he wore during the attack. The same memory is present when he is scanning faces and posture for threat, and the night-time alertness means he starts the next workday already worn down. At home, his wife sees him as tense and preoccupied, especially after days spent on exposed roofs where the environment itself resembles the setting of the attack.

Violations to fix:
- Triggers → Threat: The vignette says he studies faces and body language for signs that someone may be a threat, but it does not say triggers cause threat in a single sentence with explicit causal language.
  quote: "No single sentence explicitly states that triggers cause threat."
- Triggers → Negative Appraisals: Negative Appraisals is inactive and does not appear in the vignette. There is no sentence linking triggers to negative appraisals causally.
  quote: "No sentence contains Negative Appraisals, so this edge is absent."
- Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies: Negative Appraisals is inactive and absent from the vignette, so this edge cannot be present.
  quote: "No sentence contains Negative Appraisals, so this edge is absent."
- Negative Appraisals → Threat: Negative Appraisals is inactive and absent from the vignette, so this edge cannot be present.
  quote: "No sentence contains Negative Appraisals, so this edge is absent."
- Negative Appraisals → Memory: Negative Appraisals is inactive and absent from the vignette, so this edge cannot be present.
  quote: "No sentence contains Negative Appraisals, so this edge is absent."
- Negative Appraisals → Triggers: Negative Appraisals is inactive and absent from the vignette, so this edge cannot be present.
  quote: "No sentence contains Negative Appraisals, so this edge is absent."
- Memory → Threat: The vignette does not state that memory causes threat with explicit causal language in a single sentence.
  quote: "No single sentence explicitly states that memory causes threat."
- Memory → Negative Appraisals: Negative Appraisals is inactive and absent from the vignette, so this edge cannot be present.
  quote: "No sentence contains Negative Appraisals, so this edge is absent."
- Threat → Maladaptive Strategies: The vignette shows threat scanning and sleep-related alertness, but does not explicitly say threat causes maladaptive strategies in a single sentence with causal language.
  quote: "No single sentence explicitly states that threat causes maladaptive strategies."
- Threat → Negative Appraisals: Negative Appraisals is inactive and absent from the vignette, so this edge cannot be present.
  quote: "No sentence contains Negative Appraisals, so this edge is absent."
- Threat → Triggers: The vignette does not state that threat causes triggers with explicit causal language in a single sentence.
  quote: "No single sentence explicitly states that threat causes triggers."
- Maladaptive Strategies → Memory: The vignette does not say maladaptive strategies cause memory in a single sentence with explicit causal language; instead it says memory leads to strategies, which is the opposite direction.
  quote: ""Once those fragments are present, Daniel becomes more likely to stay mentally busy and to avoid reminders...""
- Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals: Negative Appraisals is inactive and absent from the vignette, so this edge cannot be present.
  quote: "No sentence contains Negative Appraisals, so this edge is absent."

How to fix violations:
- A violation exists only when EXPLICIT causal language directly connects a forbidden pair — words like "because of", "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by".
- Narrative proximity is NOT a violation. Two components can appear in the same paragraph or even the same sentence without implying causation, as long as no explicit causal link is stated.
- To remove a violation: delete or rephrase the explicit causal language. You do NOT need to remove the components themselves — just decouple them.
- You may restructure entire paragraphs if needed. Do not aim for minimal edits if the structure itself is the problem.
- Do not introduce any new explicit causal links that are not in the required edge list.

Daniel is a 43-year-old European roofer, married, who reports persistent post-traumatic stress after a terrorist attack. He was working near a public outdoor area when the attack happened, and he recalls the peak moment as sudden violence, objects being thrown, and the hard pounding of his own heart while he tried to get away and help others move to safety. Open natural spaces remain difficult for him, especially the kind of outdoor settings he encounters on roofing jobs and when moving between sites. He is also unsettled by seeing objects thrown in ordinary situations and by noticing his own heart racing.

When Daniel speaks about the attack, his account comes in fragments. He describes “pictures” rather than a story, with flashes of bodies moving, an object in the air, and the sound of the event hanging in his head. He says he cannot put the frightening event into words. When he is reminded of it, he sometimes hears the sounds again in his mind.

Daniel’s stuck point is close to: “That day showed me how fast things can turn dangerous, and I have to stay ready because people can act without warning.”

He studies people’s faces and body language for signs that someone may be a threat. This is noticeable at work, where he shares roofs, scaffolding, and site spaces with other tradesmen, and in the evenings at home when he is trying to settle. He has difficulty falling asleep because he needs to stay alert. He keeps his mind occupied all the time. He avoids wearing similar clothes to what he had on that day. Daniel often dwells on what other people have done to him.

The pattern is repetitive and exhausting. Natural settings, thrown objects, and the feel of his own racing heart bring the memory close in vivid sensory pieces. Those fragments sit alongside his scanning for threat, his difficulty settling at night, and his constant mental busyness. His work is affected too: on exposed roofs and at shared site meetings, he remains watchful and tense, and his wife notices that he comes home preoccupied and tired.

output:
Daniel is a 43-year-old European roofer, married, who reports persistent post-traumatic stress after a terrorist attack. He was working near a public outdoor area when the attack happened, and he recalls the peak moment as sudden violence, objects being thrown, and the hard pounding of his own heart while he tried to get away and help others move to safety. Open natural spaces remain difficult for him, especially the kind of outdoor settings he encounters on roofing jobs and when moving between sites. He is also unsettled by seeing objects thrown in ordinary situations and by noticing his own heart racing.

When Daniel speaks about the attack, his account comes in fragments. He describes “pictures” rather than a story, with flashes of bodies moving, an object in the air, and the sound of the event hanging in his head. He says he cannot put the frightening event into words. When he is reminded of it, he sometimes hears the sounds again in his mind.

Daniel’s stuck point is close to: “That day showed me how fast things can turn dangerous, and I have to stay ready because people can act without warning.”

He studies people’s faces and body language for signs that someone may be a threat. This is noticeable at work, where he shares roofs, scaffolding, and site spaces with other tradesmen, and in the evenings at home when he is trying to settle. He has difficulty falling asleep because he needs to stay alert. He keeps his mind occupied all the time. He avoids wearing similar clothes to what he had on that day. Daniel often dwells on what other people have done to him.

The pattern is repetitive and exhausting. Natural settings, thrown objects, and the feel of his own racing heart bring the memory close in vivid sensory pieces. Those fragments sit alongside his scanning for threat, his difficulty settling at night, and his constant mental busyness. His work is affected too: on exposed roofs and at shared site meetings, he remains watchful and tense, and his wife notices that he comes home preoccupied and tired.
